notes: Analyzed 291 Swift files. SwiftLint provides deterministic cyclomatic complexity scores. 1 major finding: ProcessSpawner (complexity 15). 6 minor cyclomatic violations in the 10-14 range. 12 production files exceed 500 lines (minor); SettingsView.swift at 897 lines is the largest concern. No critical complexity violations (no function reached 20+ or file reached 1000+). The large-file pattern is concentrated in SwiftUI views, which tend to inflate line counts via @ViewBuilder closures — splitting these requires care to preserve SwiftUI's state propagation semantics.

findings.title: Cyclomatic complexity 15 in ProcessSpawner
findings.description: The posix_spawn wrapper function has cyclomatic complexity 15 (major threshold: 15-19). It orchestrates POSIX file actions, spawn attributes, argv/envp array construction, and process group setup in one function — each with multiple guard/defer/free chains. High complexity here increases the risk of resource-leak bugs.
findings.snippet: // posixSpawn function starting at line 33 with nested guard/if chains for fa_init, attr_init, argv/envp construction
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Reduce cyclomatic complexity in posixSpawn function

## Context
The `posixSpawn` function in `ProcessSpawner.swift` has cyclomatic complexity 15 (tenet major threshold: 15-19). It combines POSIX file-action setup, spawn attribute configuration, argv/envp array construction, and posix_spawn invocation — each phase with its own error-check branches.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/ProcessSpawner.swift
- Line: 33
- Dimension: complexity / major

## Required change
1. Extract argv/envp construction into a dedicated `buildCStringArray(_ strings: [String]) throws -> [UnsafeMutablePointer<CChar>?]` helper that handles the strdup/free resource lifecycle.
2. Extract POSIX file-action setup into `configureFileActions(_ fa: posix_spawn_file_actions_t, pipes: SpawnPipes) throws`.
3. Extract spawn attribute setup into `configureSpawnAttributes(_ attr: posix_spawnattr_t) throws`.
4. The main function becomes: build arrays → configure fa → configure attr → call posix_spawn → await termination.

## Constraints
- The helper functions must not change the observable behavior of the spawn: same process group, same fd redirection, same argv/envp.
- Memory management (strdup/free) must remain correct — use defer blocks in the extracted helpers.
- All tests in `ProcessSpawnerTests` (if any) must still pass.

## Verification
- Run: `swiftlint lint --config .swiftlint.yml Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/ProcessSpawner.swift` — should report 0 cyclomatic_complexity violations.
- Run: `swift test --filter ProcessSpawner`.

findings.title: Cyclomatic complexity 14 in MCPSSETransport
findings.description: MCPSSETransport has a function with cyclomatic complexity 14. The SSE transport handles connection setup, bearer-auth, event dispatch, and error recovery in interleaved branches.
findings.snippet: // function at line 102 — handles SSE connection lifecycle with ~14 distinct paths
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Reduce cyclomatic complexity in MCPSSETransport

## Context
A function in MCPSSETransport.swift:102 has cyclomatic complexity 14. For an MCP server transport, high complexity here risks edge-case connection-handling bugs.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/MCP/MCPSSETransport.swift
- Line: 102
- Dimension: complexity / minor

## Required change
Extract the auth-check path into a dedicated `authorize(connection:) -> AuthResult` function, and extract per-event dispatch into `handleIncomingData(_ data: Data, connection: NWConnection) async throws`. Use early returns for error paths to flatten the nesting.

## Constraints
- Do not change the NWListener setup or the async concurrency structure.
- Preserve the 401 response path for unauthorized connections.

## Verification
- Run: `swiftlint lint Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/MCP/MCPSSETransport.swift` — cyclomatic_complexity violations should clear.
- Run: `swift test --filter MCPSSETransportTests`.

findings.title: Cyclomatic complexity 14 in RuleViewerView / ConditionEvaluator
findings.description: RuleViewerView:327 (complexity 14) and ConditionEvaluator:78 (complexity 14) both exceed the minor threshold. ConditionEvaluator's high complexity is expected (it evaluates rule conditions), but a lookup table or visitor pattern would reduce branch count significantly.
findings.snippet: // RuleViewerView:327 and ConditionEvaluator:78 — both at complexity 14
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Reduce cyclomatic complexity in ConditionEvaluator

## Context
ConditionEvaluator.swift:78 has cyclomatic complexity 14. Rule condition evaluation with a large switch or if-chain is a common pattern; replacing it with a dispatch table reduces complexity significantly.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/ConditionEvaluator.swift
- Line: 78
- Dimension: complexity / minor

## Required change
Replace the large switch/if chain with a dictionary of evaluator closures keyed by condition type. Each evaluator closure handles exactly one condition type and returns a Bool.

```swift
static let evaluators: [ConditionType: (Condition, Context) -> Bool] = [
    .fileExtension: evaluateExtension,
    .fileSize:      evaluateSize,
    // ...
]

func evaluate(_ condition: Condition, context: Context) -> Bool {
    evaluators[condition.type]?(condition, context) ?? false
}
```

## Constraints
- All condition types currently handled must continue to work.
- The public `evaluate(_:context:)` signature must not change.

## Verification
- Run: `swift test --filter ConditionEvaluatorTests`
- Run: `swiftlint lint Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/ConditionEvaluator.swift`

findings.title: 4 additional functions with cyclomatic complexity 11-14
findings.description: 4 more functions between complexity 11-14: main.swift:353 (14), EventHorizonConsoleView:123 (11), MCPCleanToolHandler:123 (11). All are minor violations. The MCP main.swift complexity stems from CLI argument parsing; the view complexity from SwiftUI conditional rendering.
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Reduce complexity in MCP main.swift CLI argument parsing

## Context
main.swift:353 has cyclomatic complexity 14 from CLI argument dispatch. CLI parsers with many flags tend to grow as features are added.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaMCP/main.swift
- Line: 353
- Dimension: complexity / minor

## Required change
Consider migrating CLI argument parsing to ArgumentParser (already available as a transitive dep via swift-tools-support-core or add it directly). ArgumentParser uses declarative syntax that reduces manual branching to near zero and generates help automatically.

Alternatively, extract each argument handler into a dedicated named function.

## Constraints
- Preserve all existing CLI flags and their semantics.

## Verification
- Run: `swift run GargantuaMCP --help` — all flags documented and functional.

findings.title: SettingsView.swift is 897 lines
findings.description: SettingsView.swift at 897 lines is the largest production file. A single SwiftUI View struct with 742 non-blank/non-comment lines makes it difficult to navigate and onboard new contributors to specific settings panes.
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Split SettingsView.swift into per-pane files

## Context
SettingsView.swift is 897 lines. SwiftUI settings screens are natural candidates for file splitting since each settings pane (General, MCP, Whitelist, etc.) is an independent SwiftUI view with its own @State and @Binding variables.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaCore/Views/SettingsView.swift
- Line: 1
- Dimension: complexity / minor

## Required change
1. Identify each tab/pane in the TabView (General, MCP, Whitelist, etc.).
2. Extract each pane into its own file: `SettingsGeneralPane.swift`, `SettingsMCPPane.swift`, `SettingsWhitelistPane.swift`, etc.
3. SettingsView.swift becomes a thin coordinator: the TabView + tab labels only.
4. Target: SettingsView.swift under 100 lines; each pane file under 300 lines.

## Constraints
- Preserve all @AppStorage, @State, @Binding, and @EnvironmentObject annotations.
- Do not change any settings key names (AppStorage keys) — these persist user data.
- Pane files should live alongside the parent in Sources/GargantuaCore/Views/Settings/.

## Verification
- Run: `swift build` to confirm no type errors.
- Run: `swift test` to confirm tests pass.
- Open the app and verify all settings panes render correctly.

findings.title: ScanBucketView.swift is 832 lines (type body 652 lines)
findings.description: ScanBucketView.swift at 832 lines contains a type body spanning 652 non-whitespace lines. This is the second-largest view. Scan result rendering, empty-state handling, and action sheets are likely separable subviews.
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Extract subviews from ScanBucketView

## Context
ScanBucketView.swift is 832 lines. Large scan-result views can be split into focused subviews (row cell, empty-state, action sheet) without changing the parent's data model.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaCore/Views/ScanBucketView.swift
- Line: 73
- Dimension: complexity / minor

## Required change
1. Extract the scan-result row view into `ScanResultRowView.swift`.
2. Extract the empty-state placeholder into `ScanBucketEmptyView.swift`.
3. Extract any action sheets / confirmation dialogs into local subview structs.
4. Keep all @Binding and state coordination in the parent ScanBucketView.

## Constraints
- Preserve @Binding connections for parent-driven state.
- Keep extracted views in the same Sources/GargantuaCore/Views/ directory.

## Verification
- Run: `swift build && swift test`.

findings.title: 10 more production files between 500-832 lines
findings.description: 10 additional production source files fall in the 500-832 line range (minor threshold 500+): DashboardView.swift (680), RuleViewerView.swift (626), SmartUninstallerViewModel.swift (601), DevArtifactScanView.swift (588), ScheduledScanService.swift (564), DiskExplorerView.swift (563), MCPRequestDispatcher.swift (538), ModelDownloadManager.swift (534), NativeScanAdapter.swift (520), MCPSSETransport.swift (508). None are critical (no file exceeds 1000 lines).
findings.snippet: // 10 files: DashboardView, RuleViewerView, SmartUninstallerViewModel, DevArtifactScanView, ScheduledScanService, DiskExplorerView, MCPRequestDispatcher, ModelDownloadManager, NativeScanAdapter, MCPSSETransport
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Incrementally split large production files as they are touched

## Context
10 production files are in the 500-832 line range. Rather than a single large refactor, a sustainable approach is to split a file whenever it is touched during normal feature work.

## Required change
For each file: identify the dominant subcomponent (e.g., a large inner view, a large switch block, a cluster of related helper methods) and extract it into a dedicated file. No single split should take more than 30 minutes.

Priority order (by line count): DashboardView → RuleViewerView → SmartUninstallerViewModel → DevArtifactScanView → ScheduledScanService → DiskExplorerView → MCPRequestDispatcher → ModelDownloadManager → NativeScanAdapter → MCPSSETransport.

## Constraints
- Only split when touching a file for another reason. Do not do a large-scale refactor.
- Each extracted file should have a clear single responsibility.

## Verification
- Run: `swift build` after each split.

findings.title: MCPCleanToolHandler function body is 82 lines
findings.description: A function in MCPCleanToolHandler.swift at line 123 spans 82 lines of code, slightly over the 80-line warning threshold. Combined with its cyclomatic complexity of 11, this is the highest-priority refactoring target in the MCP layer.
findings.fix_prompt:
# Fix: Split long function in MCPCleanToolHandler

## Context
MCPCleanToolHandler.swift:123 is an 82-line function with cyclomatic complexity 11. It handles cleanup tool dispatch but is doing validation, execution, and response formatting in one body.

## Location
- File: Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/MCP/MCPCleanToolHandler.swift
- Line: 123
- Dimension: complexity / minor

## Required change
1. Extract input validation into `validateCleanRequest(_ params: CleanParams) throws -> ValidatedRequest`.
2. Extract cleanup execution into `executeClean(_ request: ValidatedRequest) async throws -> CleanResult`.
3. Extract response formatting into `buildCleanResponse(_ result: CleanResult) -> MCPToolResponse`.
4. The main function becomes a 3-line pipeline.

## Constraints
- Do not change the function's external signature or return type.
- Preserve all error handling — the validation function should throw the same errors the current function throws.

## Verification
- Run: `swift test --filter MCPCleanToolHandlerTests`
- Run: `swiftlint lint Sources/GargantuaCore/Services/MCP/MCPCleanToolHandler.swift`
